timeline.event: Born as the daughter of Li Gefei
Married Zhao Mingcheng
First separation from Li Qingzhao as Zhao Mingcheng was implicated in the Yuanyou faction incident
Fall of the Northern Song regime
Bereaved of Zhao Mingcheng
Remarried to Zhang Ruzhou
Died

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of Jinshi Lu (Records of Bronze and Stone)
keyAchievements.description: Researched bronze and stone inscriptions and paintings with her husband Zhao Mingcheng and compiled 'Jinshi Lu'.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Song Ci (Song dynasty poetry)
keyAchievements.description: Showcased brilliant expressions full of wit with elegant works that followed the traditions of Li Yu and Yan Jidao.

keyAchievements.title: Critiques on Song dynasty Ci poets
keyAchievements.description: Known for providing apt critiques of Song dynasty Ci poets.

faq.question: Who was Li Qingzhao?
faq.answer: Li Qingzhao was a Chinese poet of the Southern Song dynasty, also known by her pseudonym Yi'an Jushi, born in 1081 during the late Northern Song period.

faq.question: What are Li Qingzhao's major achievements?
faq.answer: She compiled 'Jinshi Lu' with her husband Zhao Mingcheng, and achieved literary fame by creating Song Ci known for their elegant and witty expressions.

faq.question: How did Li Qingzhao's life change?
faq.answer: Her life is divided into early and late periods marked by the fall of the Northern Song. In the early period, she wrote happy and romantic works, while in the late period, she wrote works strongly expressing the historical and personal tragedies of the era.

timeline.event: Born
Created the masterpieces 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana'
Created the masterpiece 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto' (Old pond / a frog jumps in / sound of water)
Died (November 28, lunar October 12)

keyAchievements.title: Recognition of Haikai-no-renga style
keyAchievements.description: His works were recognized during his lifetime in the collaborative haikai-no-renga style.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Haiku
keyAchievements.description: Today, he is considered a master of haiku, which was called hokku during his time.

keyAchievements.title: Internationally renowned poet
keyAchievements.description: His poetry is internationally renowned, and many of his poems in Japan are reproduced at monuments or historical sites.

keyAchievements.title: Fame across Japan
keyAchievements.description: He entered the world of poetry at a young age, and by integrating himself into the intellectual landscape of Edo, he became famous throughout Japan.

keyAchievements.title: Inspiration through travel
keyAchievements.description: He wandered throughout Japan, including the north, east, and west, to gain inspiration for his works.

keyAchievements.title: Poetry based on experiences of nature
keyAchievements.description: His poetry was influenced by his direct experiences of the world around him, condensing emotions about landscapes into simple elements.

faq.question: Who was Matsuo Basho?
faq.answer: Matsuo Basho, born in 1644 and died on November 28, 1694 (lunar October 12), was a poet of the early Edo period. His childhood name was Kinsaku, and he was also known as Matsuo Chuemon Munefusa.

faq.question: What are Matsuo Basho's major achievements?
faq.answer: During his lifetime, his works were recognized in the collaborative haikai-no-renga style, and today he is considered a master of haiku. His poetry is internationally renowned and reproduced at monuments and historical sites across Japan.

faq.question: What are Matsuo Basho's representative works?
faq.answer: Representative works include 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana' from 1685, and 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto' from 1686.

faq.question: How did Matsuo Basho conduct his literary activities?
faq.answer: While making a living as a teacher, he distanced himself from the social and urban life where literary circles were based, wandering throughout Japan to gain inspiration. His poetry was influenced by direct experiences of the surrounding world, condensing his emotions about landscapes.

faq.question: Which of his works did Matsuo Basho consider most important?
faq.answer: He believed his best work lay in participating in and leading renku, and considered the connection with haikai verses more important than hokku.

timeline.event: Birth of Francois-Dominique Toussaint (Toussaint Louverture)
Hearing news of the French Revolution, black slaves demand the application of the principles of 'Liberty, Equality, Fraternity' in Saint-Domingue
A slave revolution breaks out in the French colony of Saint-Domingue, and Toussaint becomes one of its leaders
Leads to an agreement where France abolishes slavery in the colony
Successfully defends against invasions by British and Spanish forces
The National Convention of the French First Republic declares the abolition of black slavery in all colonies
Governs the colony with the consent of the French governor
Enacts a constitution to permanently abolish slavery and declares that all people are equal regardless of skin color
Napoleon I sends a large army to regain control of the colony and reinstate slavery
Toussaint is captured after agreeing to attend peace talks
Dies in prison while incarcerated in France
The people, inheriting Toussaint's spirit, liberate Port-au-Prince and adopt a declaration of independence
The Republic of Haiti is declared

keyAchievements.title: Led the Haitian Independence Movement
keyAchievements.description: Ended slavery and led the movement for the independence of Saint-Domingue (now Haiti) from France.

keyAchievements.title: Served as French General and Colonial Ruler
keyAchievements.description: Served as a general in the French army and colonial ruler of Saint-Domingue.

keyAchievements.title: Led the Slave Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Was one of the leaders of the slave revolution that occurred in Saint-Domingue in 1791.

keyAchievements.title: Led negotiations for the abolition of slavery
keyAchievements.description: Led an agreement in which France abolished slavery in the colony on the condition that he defeated the British and Spanish forces.

keyAchievements.title: Enacted Constitution and declared abolition of slavery
keyAchievements.description: Enacted a constitution in 1801 that abolished slavery forever and declared all people equal regardless of skin color.

faq.question: Who was Toussaint Louverture?
faq.answer: He was a Haitian revolutionary and independence fighter who ended slavery and led the independence movement of Saint-Domingue (now Haiti) from France.

faq.question: What is Toussaint Louverture's real name?
faq.answer: It is Francois-Dominique Toussaint.

faq.question: How did Toussaint Louverture become a revolutionary?
faq.answer: He became a revolutionary by teaching himself French and reading the books of Enlightenment thinkers such as Voltaire.

faq.question: When did the slave revolution led by Toussaint Louverture occur?
faq.answer: It occurred on August 22, 1791, in the French colony of Saint-Domingue.

faq.question: How did Toussaint Louverture die?
faq.answer: In late 1802, he was captured after responding to a peace talk proposal from the French commander, was imprisoned in France, and died in prison on April 7, 1803.

faq.question: What happened to Haiti after Toussaint Louverture's death?
faq.answer: Jean-Jacques Dessalines became a revolutionary and inherited Toussaint's spirit, liberated Port-au-Prince and adopted a declaration of independence in 1803, and declared the Republic of Haiti in 1804.

timeline.event: Went to study in Spain to become a doctor
Completed the final draft of the novel 'Noli Me Tangere' in Berlin in February
Formed La Liga Filipina, a leading organization for the Philippine independence movement
Arrested by the Spanish colonial government during La Liga Filipina activities and exiled to Dapitan in Mindanao
Identified as the mastermind behind the Philippine Revolution and publicly executed by firing squad by the Spanish colonial army in Bagumbayan near Manila on December 30

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'Noli Me Tangere'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote the novel 'Noli Me Tangere', which criticized the contradictions of colonial rule and depicted the discrimination of Filipinos by Spaniards.

keyAchievements.title: Formed the Philippine National League
keyAchievements.description: Formed the Philippine National League to criticize Spanish colonial rule out of a desire for Philippine liberation.

keyAchievements.title: Formed La Liga Filipina
keyAchievements.description: Directed the independence movement by forming La Liga Filipina, the leading organization for the Philippine independence movement.

faq.question: Who was Jose Rizal?
faq.answer: He was a doctor, writer, poet, journalist, educator, and a Philippine independence activist.

faq.question: Why was Jose Rizal executed?
faq.answer: He was publicly executed by firing squad by the Spanish colonial army after being identified as the mastermind behind the 1896 Philippine Revolution.

faq.question: What are Jose Rizal's representative works?
faq.answer: He is the author of 'Noli Me Tangere' and 'El Filibusterismo', and left many poems and essays.

faq.question: How is Jose Rizal remembered in the Philippines?
faq.answer: He is revered as the father of Philippine independence, and December 30, the day of his death, is commemorated as Rizal Day.

timeline.event: Born as the second child of Edward John Wollstonecraft and Elizabeth Dickson in Spitalfields, London
Became independent and worked as a companion to Sarah Dawson in Bath, Somerset
Returned home to care for her mother on her deathbed
Persuaded her sister Eliza, who was suffering from postpartum depression, to emancipate herself from her household
Left her school and followed her friend Fanny Blood to Lisbon to nurse her
Published 'Thoughts on the Education of Daughters'; decided to become an author
Published her first novel 'Mary: A Fiction'; published her only children's book 'Original Stories from Real Life'
Wrote 'A Vindication of the Rights of Men' in response to Edmund Burke's 'Reflections on the Revolution in France'
Wrote her most famous and influential work 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman'; left for Paris in December and returned a month before Louis XVI's execution
France declared war on Britain
The Jacobin-led Committee of Public Safety seized power in France
Fell in love with American adventurer Gilbert Imlay; Imlay falsely registered Wollstonecraft as his wife to obtain American citizenship
Gave birth to her first daughter, Fanny Imlay
The Jacobins fell from power
'An Historical and Moral View of the French Revolution', written in Le Havre, Northern France, was published in London
Left France
Returned to London looking for Imlay, but Imlay refused to reconcile
Attempted suicide with laudanum but was saved by Imlay
Letters written to Imlay during her Scandinavian trip were compiled and published as 'Letters Written During a Short Residence in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark'
Moved to Somers Town, northwest London, with William Godwin
Gave birth to her second daughter, Mary
Died of puerperal fever (at age 38)
Her unfinished work 'Maria' was published posthumously
Her husband William Godwin published 'Memoirs of the Author of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman'

keyAchievements.title: 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman' (1792)
keyAchievements.description: The first feminist philosophical work arguing that women are not naturally inferior to men, but appear to be only because they lack education, advocating for women's right to education and equal fundamental rights.

keyAchievements.title: 'A Vindication of the Rights of Men' (1790)
keyAchievements.description: A rebuttal to Edmund Burke's 'Reflections on the Revolution in France', and the first political and feminist writing to attack aristocracy, defend republicanism, and criticize Burke's gender-biased language.

keyAchievements.title: 'An Historical and Moral View of the French Revolution' (1794)
keyAchievements.description: A book that outlined the early history of the French Revolution, countered the counter-revolutionary atmosphere in Britain, and argued that the revolution was the result of social, economic, and political crises.

keyAchievements.title: 'Letters Written in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark' (1796)
keyAchievements.description: A travelogue containing reflections on the society and people of Scandinavia, questions of identity, and her relationship with Imlay, exploring the value of subjective experience and imagination, and advocating for women's liberation and education.

keyAchievements.title: Educational writings
keyAchievements.description: Argued through 'Thoughts on the Education of Daughters' (1787) and children's literature 'Original Stories from Real Life' (1788) that children should be taught to think and receive an education that fosters middle-class ethos.

faq.question: What is Mary Wollstonecraft's most famous book?
faq.answer: Her most famous book is 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman', published in 1792.

faq.question: What did Mary Wollstonecraft argue about women's education?
faq.answer: She argued that women are not inherently inferior to men, but only appear to be due to a lack of education, and therefore should receive quality education.

faq.question: Are any of Mary Wollstonecraft's daughters famous?
faq.answer: Yes, her second daughter, Mary Wollstonecraft Godwin, later married Percy Bysshe Shelley and became Mary Shelley, the author of the novel 'Frankenstein'.

faq.question: With which philosophers did Mary Wollstonecraft associate?
faq.answer: She associated with radical writer Thomas Paine, philosopher William Godwin, and others, and notably married William Godwin.

faq.question: What was Mary Wollstonecraft's stance on the French Revolution?
faq.answer: She condemned the Jacobin regime and the Reign of Terror, but maintained the position that the revolution was a great achievement.

timeline.event: Born as the third son of Estevao da Gama in Sines, Portugal
Joined the Order of Santiago, of which Crown Prince John (later John II) was the master
Dispatched to the Algarve by order of John II to resolve a conflict with France
Departed from Belém, Lisbon for the first expedition to India
Began staying on an island near Mozambique
Arrived in Mombasa
Arrived in Malindi
Left Malindi for India with a new navigator
Arrived in Calicut, India
Left Calicut and embarked on the return journey
Departed India
Arrived in Malindi
Returned to Lisbon
Departed for India leading the vanguard of the second Indian expedition
Arrived in Mozambique, established a trading post, and signed a trade agreement with Sofala
Arrived at Anjediva Island near Goa, India
Captured the Islamic pilgrim ship Miri and committed a massacre
Moved to Cannanore to negotiate trade
Arrived off the coast of Calicut
Indiscriminately bombarded the port of Calicut
Left India and embarked on the return journey with a fleet loaded with spices
Arrived in Lisbon
Appointed Viceroy of India, left Lisbon in April, and arrived in India in September (third voyage)
Granted the title of Count of Vidigueira by King Manuel I for his contribution to pioneering new sea routes
Appointed policy advisor by John III
Died of malaria in Cochin, India

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered the sea route to India
keyAchievements.description: Following Bartolomeu Dias's discovery of the Cape of Good Hope, he pioneered the sea route to India, a long-cherished dream of Portugal.

keyAchievements.title: First European to pioneer a sea route to India
keyAchievements.description: Sailed to India across the Atlantic Ocean and the southern coast of Africa for the first time as a European.

keyAchievements.title: Awarded the title Count of Vidigueira
keyAchievements.description: Recognized for his contribution to pioneering new sea routes, he was awarded the title Count of Vidigueira by King Manuel I in 1519.

keyAchievements.title: A total of 3 voyages to India
keyAchievements.description: Sailed to India a total of three times during his lifetime.

keyAchievements.title: Appointed Viceroy of India
keyAchievements.description: Was appointed Viceroy of India in 1514 and served as Viceroy until his death in 1524.

faq.question: When was Vasco da Gama born?
faq.answer: Vasco da Gama was born in 1469 in Sines, in the Alentejo region of southwestern Portugal.

faq.question: What are Vasco da Gama's main achievements?
faq.answer: Vasco da Gama was the first European to sail to India across the Atlantic Ocean and the southern coast of Africa, pioneering the sea route to India.

faq.question: How many times did Vasco da Gama sail to India?
faq.answer: Vasco da Gama sailed to India a total of three times during his lifetime.

faq.question: Where did Vasco da Gama die?
faq.answer: Vasco da Gama died of malaria in Cochin, India on December 24, 1524.

faq.question: How long did Vasco da Gama's first voyage to India take?
faq.answer: Vasco da Gama's first voyage to India took a total of two years, from leaving Lisbon in July 1497 until returning.

timeline.event: Birth
Volunteered as an envoy to form an alliance with the Dayuezhi against the Xiongnu for the Han Dynasty and departed for the Western Regions
Returned to the Han Dynasty
Contributed to Emperor Wu's attack on the Xiongnu and was appointed Weiwei (Commandant of Guards) and Marquis of Bowang
Demoted to commoner after being held responsible for missing the deadline for a military campaign
Dispatched as an envoy to Wusun
Death

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered the Silk Road
keyAchievements.description: Made a significant contribution to the pioneering of the Silk Road.

keyAchievements.title: Transmitted knowledge of the Western Regions
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to establishing new strategies by transmitting valuable knowledge about the Western Regions to the Han Dynasty.

keyAchievements.title: Contributed to attacks against the Xiongnu
keyAchievements.description: Made significant contributions during Emperor Wu's attacks against the Xiongnu in 123 BC by utilizing his experience and geographical knowledge.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed alliance with Wusun
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for improved relations between the Han Dynasty and Wusun by proposing a marriage alliance and eastward migration.

faq.question: Who was Zhang Qian?
faq.answer: Zhang Qian was a traveler, diplomat, and explorer during the Han Dynasty of China in the 2nd century BC, who made significant contributions to pioneering the Silk Road.

faq.question: What was the main purpose of Zhang Qian's dispatch to the Western Regions?
faq.answer: The Han Dynasty recruited an envoy to form an alliance with the Dayuezhi against the Xiongnu, and Zhang Qian volunteered and headed for the Dayuezhi.

faq.question: What was the biggest outcome Zhang Qian's exploration of the Western Regions brought to the Han Dynasty?
faq.answer: Although the alliance was not established, the valuable knowledge about the Western Regions brought by Zhang Qian contributed significantly to the Han Dynasty formulating new strategies.

faq.question: What items is Zhang Qian said to have brought back from the Western Regions?
faq.answer: Legends exist that he brought back items such as grapes, pomegranates, and peaches from the Western Regions.

timeline.event: Born to Pepin the Short and Bertrada of Laon in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.
His father, Pepin the Short, became King of the Franks with the approval of Pope Zachary.
Pope Stephen II bestowed the title of King on Pepin and his sons Charles and Carloman.
Succeeded to the throne after the death of his father Pepin the Short, taking over Austrasia and the northern regions.
Became sole ruler after the death of his brother Carloman I.
Began attacking the Saxons.
Destroyed and annexed the Lombard Kingdom in northern Italy.
Attended the Easter festival in Rome with the Pope and reaffirmed the promise to transfer the Papal States.
Suffered a crushing defeat by the Basques at the Battle of Roncevaux Pass during an expedition to the Iberian Peninsula.
Visited Rome, had his two sons Pepin and Louis consecrated, and bestowed upon them the crowns of Lombardy and Aquitaine respectively.
His wife Hildegard and mother Bertrada of Laon passed away successively, and he remarried Fastrada.
Conquered Thuringia and Bavaria, and deposed Duke Tassilo III of Bavaria.
Soundly defeated the Avars along the Danube River and drove them beyond the borders.
Built a palace and royal church in Aachen, northwestern Germany, making it the de facto capital.
Pope Leo III, attacked by opponents, fled to Charlemagne's court to seek assistance.
Was crowned Emperor of the Romans by Pope Leo III in St. Peter's Basilica.
Louis the Pious captured Barcelona.
The Avar Khagan Zodan visited Aachen, swore fealty to Charlemagne, and converted to Christianity.
Croatian chiefs, including Viseslav, voluntarily surrendered.
Eastern Roman Emperor Michael I Rangabe recognized Charlemagne as Emperor.
Declared his surviving son Louis I the Pious as the legitimate successor at the court in Aachen and crowned him co-emperor.
Died of complications from pleurisy and depression at the Palace of Aachen.
Pope Paschal III canonized Charlemagne as a saint at the request of Frederick I.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of the Frankish Kingdom
keyAchievements.description: Greatly expanded the Frankish Kingdom by conquering most of western and central Europe, achieving the political unification of Western Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Crowned Emperor of the Romans
keyAchievements.description: Received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800, politically and religiously unifying most of Western Europe for the first time since the Roman Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Carolingian Renaissance
keyAchievements.description: Initiated the Carolingian Renaissance by greatly developing arts, religion, and culture through the church during his reign.

keyAchievements.title: Codification of laws and centralization
keyAchievements.description: Codified the laws of various tribes to rule over vast territories and enabled centralized rule by dispatching officials from the center.

keyAchievements.title: Spread and integration of Christianity
keyAchievements.description: Sought ideological unity by converting conquered tribes to Catholicism and supported proselytization by dispatching missionaries.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of cultural and educational institutions
keyAchievements.description: Led a revival of learning and arts by building a palace and royal church in Aachen, and founding a royal library and palace school.

faq.question: When was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: There are theories that Charlemagne was born on April 2 of 740 or 742, or in 747.

faq.question: Why is Charlemagne called the 'Father of Europe'?
faq.answer: He is called the 'Father of Europe' because the Carolingian Renaissance laid the foundation for the current identity of Europe.

faq.question: When did Charlemagne become Emperor of the Romans?
faq.answer: Charlemagne received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800.

faq.question: How tall was Charlemagne?
faq.answer: According to Einhard's records and the results of a tomb excavation in 1861, Charlemagne was about 190 to 191 cm tall.

faq.question: What languages did Charlemagne speak?
faq.answer: In addition to his mother tongue, he was fluent in Latin and could also speak some Greek.

faq.question: Where was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: Charlemagne was born in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.

timeline.event: Reigned as the ninth Sapa Inca of the Kingdom of Cusco and the first Emperor of the Inca Empire.
Recognized as the successor to the throne for his merit in defending Cusco during the Chanka invasion.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the foundation for the Inca Empire
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for developing the small Kingdom of Cusco into the massive Inca Empire ruling all of western South America.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of imperial territory
keyAchievements.description: During his reign, Collasuyu and Chinchaysuyu, two of the four quarters of the Inca Empire, were brought under the empire's control.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion and overhaul of the capital Cusco
keyAchievements.description: Transformed Cusco from a quiet provincial city into a place fitting as the capital of the empire through massive expansion.

keyAchievements.title: Renovation of Coricancha
keyAchievements.description: Overhauled various buildings of the Inca Empire era, including the renovation of Coricancha, the Temple of the Sun.

keyAchievements.title: Implementation of forced resettlement policy
keyAchievements.description: Implemented a colonization policy of forcibly moving residents from the center of the empire to borderlands to pioneer frontier regions.

faq.question: What does Pachacuti's name mean?
faq.answer: In Quechua, it means 'He who shakes time and space' or 'Earth Shaker', and 'Yupanqui' means 'honor'.

faq.question: What was Pachacuti's position in the Inca Empire?
faq.answer: He was the ninth Sapa Inca of the Kingdom of Cusco and the first Emperor of the Inca Empire.

faq.question: Who was Pachacuti's father?
faq.answer: His father was Viracocha Inca.

faq.question: How did Pachacuti become the successor to the throne?
faq.answer: He was recognized as the successor to the throne around 1438 for his merit in defending Cusco alone when his father and brother fled during the Chanka invasion.

faq.question: Who is presumed to have built Machu Picchu?
faq.answer: Most archaeologists presume that Pachacuti built Machu Picchu.

timeline.event: Birth of Akbar
His father Humayun reconquers India
Ascends to the throne as the 3rd Emperor of the Mughal Empire at the age of 13
Defeats the Hindu army near Delhi
Renovates the old fort in Agra and renames it 'Akbarabad'
Begins construction of the new capital Fatehpur Sikri near Agra
Fatehpur Sikri begins to be used as the capital
A massive rebellion against a series of reforms takes place
The capital is moved from Fatehpur Sikri to Lahore
Lahore serves as Akbar's capital
Death of Akbar

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Mughal Empire
keyAchievements.description: Developed the Mughal Empire founded by his grandfather Babur into a state truly worthy of being an empire.

keyAchievements.title: Seizure of power and recruitment of talent
keyAchievements.description: Seized power himself after coming of age and recruited talented people regardless of their backgrounds.

keyAchievements.title: Territorial expansion
keyAchievements.description: Allied with and pacified Rajput princes, annexed most of northern India based on a new army, and expanded the domain southwards by advancing into the Deccan plateau in his later years.

keyAchievements.title: Institutional reform
keyAchievements.description: Reformed the tax system, including abolishing the jizya (poll tax) imposed on non-Muslims under Islamic law.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of the Mansabdari system
keyAchievements.description: Introduced the Mansabdari system to secure stable military power and hierarchicalized the bureaucracy.

keyAchievements.title: Construction projects
keyAchievements.description: Completed his father Humayun's tomb, renovated the old fort in Agra naming it 'Akbarabad', and started building Fatehpur Sikri to use as the capital.

keyAchievements.title: Religious tolerance
keyAchievements.description: Paid attention to various religions and adopted a religious stance called 'Din-i Ilahi' that syncretically respected multiple religions.

keyAchievements.title: Patronage of culture and scholarship
keyAchievements.description: Although uneducated in his youth, he loved the arts and patronized scholarship, initiating translation projects from Sanskrit to Persian.

faq.question: When was Akbar the Great born?
faq.answer: Akbar was born on October 25, 1542.

faq.question: Which emperor of the Mughal Empire was Akbar the Great?
faq.answer: Akbar the Great was the 3rd Emperor of the Mughal Empire.

faq.question: When did Akbar the Great ascend to the throne?
faq.answer: Akbar ascended to the throne at the young age of 13 in 1556.

faq.question: What tax did Akbar the Great abolish?
faq.answer: Akbar abolished the jizya (poll tax) imposed on non-Muslims under Islamic law.

faq.question: What military and bureaucratic system did Akbar the Great introduce?
faq.answer: Akbar introduced the Mansabdari system to secure stable military power.

faq.question: Which major city did Akbar the Great build?
faq.answer: Akbar began the construction of a new capital, Fatehpur Sikri, near Agra.

faq.question: What was Akbar the Great's perspective on religion?
faq.answer: Akbar paid attention to various religions and possessed a religious stance called 'Din-i Ilahi' that syncretically respected multiple religions.
